content:
This lesson focuses on building sell watchlists for shorting stocks. The Dow Jones Industrial composite
stock list will be used. It's important to understand that not all index stocks move uniformly (up or down)
during bullish or bearish market days. We will concentrate on identifying conditions ideal for bearishness to
construct effective sell watchlists.

section_title: SEASONAL BEARISH FOCUS AND INITIAL FILTERS
content:
As a reminder, we will focus on the bearish months and any consolidation months for Dow Jones Industrial
seasonals. The primary focus area is from May through July.
Building sell watchlists involves two key filters:
- The stock market must be poised to decline.
- During bearish months, look for stocks that make a lower high.

section_title: IDEAL SHORT SWING SETUPS AND STOCK SELECTION
content:
The months of January and May through July are ideal for short swing setups.
- Majority of index stocks will decline with a major market decline.
- Stocks trending lower on a weekly chart prior to this setup are ideal.
- Avoid 'safe stocks' like Verizon, GE, and Coca-Cola.
- Weak stocks will exhibit obvious bearish market structure.
- Premium arrays with Index SMT will highlight companies under heavy distribution during seasonally
bearish months.
- The selection process aims to narrow down to two to four companies.
- Leadership stocks aggressively sold by institutions will fail to rally higher during bearish months,
especially when the three major indices rally until one of those indices fails to post a comparable higher
high.

section_title: FOCUSING ON MAY-JULY BEARISH PERIOD AND SMT DIVERGENCE
content:
While January is generally a bearish month, the primary focus will be on the period from the May high
through the month of July, encompassing the beginning of May to the first of August.
We will primarily focus on Index SMT divergences. For example, in the 2015 May-June-July period, indices
failed to make comparable higher highs. The NASDAQ made a higher high, but the Dow and S&P failed to,
which is a classic fingerprint for distribution in the stock market.

- 2015 SEASONAL DECLINE
The first stock example is American Express (AXP), viewed on a weekly chart. The legwork of identifying
markets with bearish institutional order flow on the weekly chart has been pre-completed.
- 2014 (Bullish Market): During a bullish May-July period, although a high was called, the Dow Jones
(green cumulative line) failed to make a lower high, meaning there was no SMT divergence for
bearishness. No obvious divergence means no divergence. There was no gravitational pull for AXP to pull
lower or fail to make a higher high.
- 2015 (Bearish Market): Between May and the first of August 2015, the Dow Jones was making higher
highs leading up to May. However, American Express shares were seeing failure to rally comparably,
indicating heavy distribution. The May-June-July period resulted in a seasonal high, and the price traded off
rather aggressively from around 80 a share down to almost 50 a share (a 30 a share decline).

- 2015 SEASONAL DECLINE
The next stock reviewed is Caterpillar (CAT).
- Bullish Market Comparison: When the market was strong, it could create a high even if bearishness was
expected. However, a divergence with the indices, specifically a failure swing to produce a lower high
during those months, was not present. Therefore, no action could be taken, as it didn't fit the specified
criteria.
- 2015 (Bearish Market): From May to August 2015, the Dow Jones was making higher highs while
Caterpillar was simultaneously failing to make a higher high. Institutional order flow on the weekly chart
was bearish. Prices moved from almost 90 a share all the way down to almost 55 a share, a decline of
approximately 45 a share. This demonstrates how index stocks can yield significant moves when these
conditions align.

- 2015 SEASONAL DECLINE
The next stock is Chevron Corporation (CVX).
- Bullish Market Comparison: In 2014, when the stock market was bullish, it did cause a high to form in
Chevron. However, the focus is on when institutional order flow is bearish.
- 2015 (Bearish Market): In May to August 2015, institutional order flow was bearish. The Dow Jones was
making higher highs, while Chevron shares were simultaneously making lower highs, indicating heavy
distribution. There was a ramp-up in selling acceleration during the seasonal decline from May to August,
leading to a huge decline from 110 a share all the way down to 70 a share.

- 2015 SEASONAL DECLINE
The next example is Exxon Mobil (XOM).
- Bullish Market Comparison: For comparison, when the market was bullish, the seasonal influence of May
to August could lead to a high in shares.
- 2015 (Bearish Market): However, the focus is on heavy distribution when institutional order flow is already
bearish, which was observed in May to August 2015. The Dow Jones was posting higher highs, while
Exxon Mobil shares were simultaneously failing to make higher highs. This resulted in an acceleration of
selling from 90 a share all the way down to almost 66 a share, a significant move.

- 2015 AND IDEAL SETUP REFINEMENT
The final stock on the list is Walmart (WMT).
- Bullish Market Comparison: During bullish market conditions, even with an expected bearish period from
May to August 1st, a slight decline was observed. However, the critical factor is a bearish market tone on a
weekly basis with bearish institutional order flow.
- 2015 (Bearish Market): In 2015, with bearish institutional order flow on the weekly chart, the Dow Jones
made higher highs while Walmart was simultaneously failing to make higher highs. This led to a significant
decline from 80 a share all the way down to almost 56 a share.
- Refinement: While Walmart's decline was impressive, it might have been set aside from the primary
selections. This is because there was prolonged consolidation in late 2013 through 2014, followed by a
rally, but then no genuine attempt to rally before the decline; it was more of a steady drift lower. For ideal
setups, you really want to find an area where price wants to rally away, similar to a 'Judas swing' or a
market protractionary state, where the rally is then sold. The previous stock selections (AXP, CAT, CVX,
XOM) would have been more ideal choices for put options due to this clearer rally failure.

section_title: THE CONSISTENCY OF INSTITUTIONAL ORDER FLOW AND TRACKING SMART MONEY
content:
This approach to timing the stock market is highly consistent because institutions operate with consistency,
following rule-based programs.
- If these generic traits and recurring conditions (like those taught in the buy and sell watchlist lessons) are
understood, it reveals the institutional program.
- This program leaves predictable footprints every year, allowing traders to track 'smart money' due to the
sheer volume of their trading, accumulation, or distribution.
- The consistency of these characteristics in price action, guided by institutional programs, suggests that
this method should remain effective for years, potentially serving as a valuable tool for future generations to
build wealth or generate additional income.

section_title: IMPORTANCE OF GENERAL MARKET INSIGHTS AND HOMEWORK
content:
It is crucial to understand these telltale signs, regardless of whether one is actively interested in trading
stocks. This knowledge provides vital insight into the general market. The four asset class studies
completed this month (stocks, bonds, commodities, S&P) are paramount for comprehensive market
understanding.
- Homework Assignment: Go through the NASDAQ 100 stocks this week. Search for 'NASDAQ 100 index
stocks' on Google to get the symbols. Although it is more than three times the amount of Dow Jones
stocks, it is for personal benefit.
- Apply the same parameters outlined in both the buy model and sell model tutorials.
- The process involves looking at a specific time of year, a condition based on institutional order flow
(bearish or bullish on the weekly chart), and aligning that with seasonal tendencies.

The remaining content for this month will cover further evaluation for stock selections, incorporating
options. A bonus lesson will also discuss how to utilize all the asset class information for maximum benefit.
Until the next lesson, good luck and good trading.
